title: RIGHT JOIN
description: The opposite of the Left Join.

slides.title: The Mirror Image 🪞
slides.content:
# Keeping the right

A **`RIGHT JOIN`** is the exact perfect opposite of a `LEFT JOIN`.

It guarantees that every single row from the "Right" table (the second one you write) survives, even if there is zero matching data in the absolute first table.

slides.title: Why use it? 🤷‍♂️
slides.content:
## To be honest... we rarely do.

In the real world, 99% of developers simply structure their queries so their primary table is written first, and then use a `LEFT JOIN`.

`users RIGHT JOIN orders` and `orders LEFT JOIN users` yield nearly identical results! We just teach it so you know it exists.

quizData.question: What does `SELECT * FROM users RIGHT JOIN orders;` guarantee?

options.text: That every single user comes back
That every single order comes back, even if it is somehow missing a user
That only perfect matches are returned

quizData.explanation: Because 'orders' is on the right side of the join statement, the RIGHT JOIN guarantees all its rows survive.

slides.title: Null Fillers Again 👻
slides.content:
## The missing pieces

Just like before, if an order survives the `RIGHT JOIN` but inherently has no matching User... what populates the `first_name` and `email` columns for that order's row?

It's padded with `NULL`s!

quizData.question: Is there any fundamental mathematical difference between A `LEFT JOIN` B, and B `RIGHT JOIN` A?

options.text: Yes, they produce completely irreconcilable tables
No, they are effectively the exact same logical operation just mirrored

quizData.explanation: They perform the same logic. Developers universally prefer standardizing on LEFT joins purely for readability.
